chapters.summary: Good morning. Before we start our official council meeting, we have a pre-gavel celebration. This is going to be my first pre-GAvel, and I'm pretty honored to get to do it for DJ OG One.
chapters.headline: Council President Angelita Murillo kicks off pre-gavel celebration
chapters.gist: Pre-City Council Meeting Celebrations

chapters.summary: November 30th is DJ OG One Day in the City of Portland. David Jackson is the official DJ for the Portland Trail Blazers. In the streets and neighborhoods of Portland, he has been a powerful voice for unity, peace, and community. City proclamations are a way for the city to publicly recognize those who shape our culture.
chapters.headline: Mayor Wilson officially recognizes November 30th as DJ OG One Day in Portland
chapters.gist: DJ OG One

chapters.summary: DJ OG One Jackson officially recognized November 30th as DJ OG One Day. Jackson: This moment is bigger than just recognizing me, but it really is about recognizing what I stand for. Encouraging people to live life with purpose and intention.
chapters.headline: Portland officially recognizes DJ OG One Day on November 30
chapters.gist: DJ OG One Day

chapters.summary: Portland City Council will meet Wednesday, March 3rd, March 11th. I'm here remotely due to an eye infection. Can we please call the rules of decorum?
chapters.headline: Council President: I'm calling to order the Wednesday, March 3rd meeting
chapters.gist: Portland City Council Meeting Order

chapters.summary: To testify before council in person or virtually, you must sign up in advance on the council agenda. Individuals may testify for 3 minutes unless the presiding officer states otherwise. Your microphone will be muted when your time is over. Further disruption will result in ejection from the meeting.
chapters.headline: To testify before Portland City Council, you must sign up in advance
chapters.gist: Portland City Council Meeting

chapters.summary: Council President: Trying to get to 17 items on a schedule is too much. Council also post postponed Slow the Flow ordinance and business income license tax issue. New ordinance tied based off of work session will be filed. Council President: It's highly inappropriate to take two ordinances and indefinitely postpone them.
chapters.headline: Council President, many of our items have been bumped today
chapters.gist: Council Approves the Agenda

chapters.summary: Monica: Despite all the money that we've poured into our community, our city continues to stumble and fall apart. Monica: It's time to expand the climate fund into an environmental cleanup fund. She asks that you understand the silent majority is paying attention.
chapters.headline: Monica says despite all the money poured into Portland, city continues to fall apart
chapters.gist: Portland's silent majority speaks out

chapters.summary: Don Jones Jr. highlights safety concerns surrounding the proposed alternate access redemption center. Increasing foot and bicycle activity in this location directly conflicts with the city's Vision Zero policies and commitments. Jones urges the mayor and the council to communicate with the Oregon Beverage Recycling Cooperative that this location is unacceptable.
chapters.headline: Portland has formally adopted Vision Zero, committing to prioritize safety for vulnerable road users
chapters.gist: Proposed location for homeless recycling center violates Vision Zero

chapters.summary: Peggy McDaniel: I'm here to raise grave safety concerns about the proposed location of the first AARC to be operated by the People's Depot. Xiao Yang Wang: We respectfully ask for 12-month pause, 6 to 12 months, to identify a site that is logical, safe, and sustainable for everyone.
chapters.headline: Peggy McDaniel raises grave safety concerns about proposed AARC location
chapters.gist: Safety concerns about the proposed location of the People's Depot AARC

chapters.summary: Shen Yun was founded in 2006 by Falun Gong practitioners, many of whom fled China for religious freedom. Its mission is to revive traditional Chinese culture, which has been destroyed under the communist rule since 1949. The Chinese Communist Party is trying to stop people from seeing the show.
chapters.headline: The Chinese Communist Party is trying to stop people from seeing Shen Yun shows
chapters.gist: Why is the CCP so afraid of Shen Yun?

chapters.summary: An appeal has been filed with the hearing officer for the notice of land use violation at the ICE facility. The Portland Bureau of Emergency Management is also developing response protocols and scenario planning for citywide federal response. I just want to keep council abreast of those key items.
chapters.headline: Council will receive updates on federal response at beginning of council meetings
chapters.gist: Portland City Council: Immigration Affairs, Emergency Preparedness

chapters.summary: Council votes to adopt the Public Infrastructure Environmental Code project to streamline environmental zoning regulations. The ordinance passes with 11 yes votes and 1 absent. Can we please call item number 6?
chapters.headline: Council approves second reading of emergency ordinance with 12 yes votes and 1 absent
chapters.gist: Portland City Council: Committee Referral List

chapters.summary: Simone Reddy is the elected City Auditor of Portland. Her office oversees city elections and enforces the city's campaign finance and lobbying regulations. An amendment would limit the auditor's discretion to extend enforcement timelines. Reddy asks that the amendment be advanced to a second reading today.
chapters.headline: Simone Reddy is the elected City Auditor of Portland
chapters.gist: Portland City Auditor Simone Reddy on the City's Campaign Finance Code

chapters.summary: Councilor Murillo moves her amendment. Councilor Kunal seconds it. Councillor Novick has a couple of nitpicky editorial suggestions. This is actually a first reading. It was continued.
chapters.headline: Council President: Colleagues, discussion to the amendments
chapters.gist: Council passes amendments to the Climate Bill

chapters.summary: Amendment to make it more fair and just for our elections passed 5-0. I'd love to hear about the workload implications of these amendments. Can anybody speak to that a little bit more?
chapters.headline: Councilor Ryan Zimmerman: I'm skeptical of amendments to election law
chapters.gist: Small Donor Elections Amendment Passes

chapters.summary: There's some concern about the level of discretion that this code provides. Does this make it easier or harder for you as the auditor to ensure the system is sticking to its origins. On the whole, I do think it's an improvement.
chapters.headline: Deborah Scroggin: I think this is an improvement
chapters.gist: Auditor on Small Donors Elections Code

chapters.summary: Councilor Murillo: It's just meant to address the problem of continuously extending the sufficiency review while also making sure that there is a thorough investigation if there's a broader issue that needs to be looked into. I'm trying to get a sense of the size of the change here.
chapters.headline: Councilor Murillo: This amendment addresses problem of continuously extending sufficiency review
chapters.gist: The 14-Day Sufficiency Review Amendment

chapters.summary: Councillor: If a complaint comes to your office 3 weeks before an election, you would have to end your sufficiency review at the latest 1 week before. That would force you to get involved in an election days before, regardless of findings. Councillor says having a set timeline removes the political decision aspect of it.
chapters.headline: Councillor says new code puts auditor in electioneering position
chapters.gist: Ontario Auditor's Code: Elections and Complaints

chapters.summary: Councilor Knaul: We need to respond to the court's ruling so that the Portland campaign finance laws remain enforceable. Councilor Connell: This amendment represents a more careful step forward. Council should take a careful and narrow approach and avoid making broader changes.
chapters.headline: Councilor Zimmerman supports adding hearing process to Portland campaign finance system
chapters.gist: Portland City Council passes Amendment to Campaign Finance Law

chapters.summary: Was there an ad package requested? Did you ask for the capacity you would need? The concerns that I've heard so far can happen under the status quo. I would urge us to move forward. And if it passes, passes.
chapters.headline: Councilor Zimmerman: Was there an ad package requested to resolve capacity concerns
chapters.gist: Councillor Maria on the Capacity Concerns

chapters.summary: Amendment says that within two calendar days of receiving a complaint, we'll issue notification to each— to every person who is the object of the complaint. Amendment is approved with 9 yes votes and 3 no votes.
chapters.headline: Council approves Murillo amendment with 9 yes votes and 3 no votes
chapters.gist: Murillo Amendment to Complaint Notice

chapters.summary: The amendment is approved with 11 yes votes and 1 no vote. In the interest of time, I withdraw my nitpicky amendments. This is the first reading, so we're voting on this next week. There's not immediate urgency, I think.
chapters.headline: The amendment is approved with 11 yes votes and 1 no vote
chapters.gist: Cannibal 1 Amendment

chapters.summary: Reed: I would love it if you could circle back to us on whether or not the due process concerns could be resolved by giving more flexibility on the sufficiency review and less on the actual investigation. The Governance Committee now moves to second reading.
chapters.headline: Reed Adams: I appreciate clarification on sufficiency review versus investigation extension
chapters.gist: Proposal to extend the sufficiency review period

chapters.summary: Report requests the appointment of 7 new members and the reappointment of 3 members to the Community Involvement Committee. No written or verbal testimony was submitted on this item prior to committee action. Second motion moved to accept the report.
chapters.headline: Council President recommends Climate Resilience and Land Use Committee be accepted
chapters.gist: Climate Committee: Appointments and Remarks

chapters.summary: Councilor Ryan: I have been a long supporter of this legislation. Unfortunately, this was scheduled for to a council meeting when I was traveling for work. I would have preferred that we have maintained the broader inclusive language of the original legislation. But I will accept the ordinance and the resolution as they are.
chapters.headline: Councilor Ryan speaks out on second reading of Portland anti-LGBTQ ordinance
chapters.gist: Portland City Council Second Reading LGBTQIA Ordinance

chapters.summary: The ordinance declares the City of Portland will defend the rights of transgender people and all of the LGBTQIA2S+ community. The ordinance passes with 11 yes votes and 1 absent.
chapters.headline: The ordinance passes with 11 yes votes and 1 absent
chapters.gist: Portland Transgender Rights Ordinance Pass

chapters.summary: Councilor Ryan: These types of resolutions evolve over the years with the topic or sometimes the threat of the day of the era. He thanks Councilor Smith for her efforts in 2011 at National Coming Out Day. Ryan: The old idea of it gets 'it gets better' is not lost on me.
chapters.headline: Gina Zimmerman: I'm reminded of National Coming Out Day in 2011
chapters.gist: Portland City Council passes National Coming Out Day Resolution

chapters.summary: In 2004, Multnomah County lost a public vote on same-sex marriage in the state. Councilor Zimmerman: One really good thing to hold on to is the progress we've made on this issue. The resolution is adopted with 12 yes votes.
chapters.headline: Councilor Zimmerman: We've come a long way on same-sex marriage
chapters.gist: Council votes to adopt same-sex marriage resolution

chapters.summary: Amend the Portland Clean Energy Community Benefit Fund Climate Investment Plan. Second reading of a non-emergency ordinance. Councilor Knoll asks if anyone can answer a question that came up in written testimony.
chapters.headline: Measure 2026-022 amend Portland Clean Energy Community Benefit Fund Climate Investment Plan
chapters.gist: Portland Clean Energy Community Benefits Fund Climate Investment Plan Second Reading

chapters.summary: Councilor Pirtle-Guinea: The James Beard Market could test bike cargo to deliver goods daily. Councilor Ryan: There's a struggle sometimes for the last council and this council to know when we're supposed to weigh in. Council President: I hope that as we move forward, we can continue to be clear about our role.
chapters.headline: Councilor Nowak supports James Beard Market bike cargo project
chapters.gist: Portland Transportation Committee Discusses James Beard Market Project

chapters.summary: Councilor Knoll: Are we removing our workforce standards in every single PCF program or just in housing development? Councilman: What is getting pulled back across our programs is essentially having these numeric goals and targets. Council is working to figure out what comes after that.
chapters.headline: Council member Sam Knoll questions whether PCF is removing workforce standards
chapters.gist: City Council: Should We Remove Our Workforce Standards in PCF

chapters.summary: Councilor Pirtle-Guinea: When do we expect the disparity study that the City of Portland is a part of to be complete? Would that apply to the Portland Housing Bureau work that is being funded through PSF? Council President: This is part of a broader conversation about complying in advance with the federal government.
chapters.headline: Councilor Smith: When do we expect the disparity study to be complete
chapters.gist: Council passes resolution to remove language from the disparity study

chapters.summary: Councilor Green suggested postponing the item to a date certain to give folks time to address the issues that were raised. Rules allow you to divide the question on final passage of an ordinance. The soonest date I would be able to get it is April 1st.
chapters.headline: Councilor Green suggests postponing item to date certain so alternative language can be drafted
chapters.gist: Council postpones Final Passage of Ordinance to a date certain

chapters.summary: Council President Don Oliveira: The language that was struck from the CIP was verbatim taken from a PHB website that was unpublished in October. Not taking anything away from the deliberations about replacing the language.
chapters.headline: Council President: I just want to clarify language struck from CIP
chapters.gist: CIP language struck from the City's website

chapters.summary: Council is currently working with procurement and the city attorney's office to re-establish that, that work. Councillor Pirtle-Guinea: I'm hearing a number of colleagues who are somewhat uncomfortable comfortable moving this forward. Council President: I would love to have something come directly to full council at that April 1st meeting or the April 8th meeting.
chapters.headline: Council President proposes moving this forward today with deletions and replacement
chapters.gist: Proposal to move this CIP language replacement forward

chapters.summary: Council President: Will that include the amendments that we also have approved, like Avalos 1? Councilor Smith: I would propose something I'm willing to do. This solution would be able to move this forward today. I really urge everyone to support this.
chapters.headline: Council President: I urge everyone to support this today
chapters.gist: Proposal to move this matter forward on Council

chapters.summary: Councilor Green suggested adding the emergency clause and removing the alteration to page 24 of the exhibit in a single motion. Councilor Pirtle-Guinea suggested a fix to the problem might have to bypass the normal PCF committee process. I'm comfortable putting this up as it is, knowing it's likely to fail.
chapters.headline: Robert Green suggests adding emergency clause and removing strikethroughs
chapters.gist: Add the Emergency Clause to the Exhibit

chapters.summary: Councilor Kunal: The amendments that were proposed are being redacted or retracted. There are no amendments formally proposed at this time. Councilor Smith: My preferred path would be to have us move to a vote of this. Would that release the money and support Avalos One?
chapters.headline: Councilor Kunal Smith retracts proposed amendments to Avalos One
chapters.gist: Councilor Kunal withdraws amendment to pass Avalos One

chapters.summary: Councilor Pirtle-Guinea asks when the results of the disparity study will be complete. Will the results be applicable to Portland Housing Bureau policy? Depends upon the funding models and the coverage of engagement.
chapters.headline: Council President asks when we expect results of the disparity study to be complete
chapters.gist: Will the disparity study be applicable to Portland Housing Bureau Contracting?

chapters.summary: Council President: We need to move forward to release the other dollars and programs that are in here. Councillor Avalos: I plan to support this. We've got to deal with all of the structural problems later, but let's not delay getting these dollars out.
chapters.headline: Council President: Let's not delay getting these dollars out
chapters.gist: Councillor Bertolini: Moving the Disparity Study forward

chapters.summary: 45 days from today is April 25th. The ordinance passes with 9 yes votes and 3 no votes. More to come on this.
chapters.headline: The ordinance passes with 9 yes votes and 3 no votes
chapters.gist: Rights to Marriage Ordinance

chapters.summary: Council President Keelan: Can we please call items 11 and 12 together? Item 11, 2026-082, approve the Colley Tax Increment Financing District 5-year action plan. Item 12, 20 26-083, appoint and reappoint members to the Collel Tax Financing Community Leadership Council.
chapters.headline: Ashley Hernandez from Council Operations provides a committee staff summary
chapters.gist: Appointments to the Colley Tax Increment Financing District

chapters.summary: Coach, I'd like to add the conversation— here I am getting the elephant out of the room— around overhead. The way we define overhead is different in different systems. Colleagues, we're moving to technical or clarifying questions.
chapters.headline: Coach, I'd like to add the conversation around overhead needs clarification
chapters.gist: Councillor Ryan: On Overhead

chapters.summary: Prosper Portland's chief financial officer, Tony Barnes, answers council's questions. Estimates are approximately 14% for program delivery and 13% for administration. As there's more capital, more proceeds recognized in the district in later years, those percentages could be adjusted downward.
chapters.headline: Tony Barnes: We define the administrative cost in 3 different buckets
chapters.gist: Administrative Cost of Prosper Portland

chapters.summary: Would you say it's fair to characterize that we should normally expect there to be a higher percentage of kind of indirect or indirect— direct or indirect overhead in that sort of ramp period relative to further into the 30-year period?
chapters.headline: Would you say there's a higher percentage of indirect overhead in ramp period
chapters.gist: Overhead for the District's 5-year Action Plan

chapters.summary: Council member: There's a lot of work that needs to happen when you're starting up TIF districts. Kali community helped set the standard for what community-led economic development can look like, he says. Council member: I'm keeping a very close eye on Cully to make sure that that progress does come to fruition.
chapters.headline: First, I'll just comment on what was just discussed by my colleagues
chapters.gist: Community-led TIF district: admin overhead

chapters.summary: Shabri Vickers: Are there any lessons from this Kali experience that you think the city should carry forward as we implement other community-led TIF districts? Amy Nagy: Any other lessons?
chapters.headline: Councilor Avalos: Were there any lessons from this Kali experience
chapters.gist: East Portland TIF: Lessons Learned

chapters.summary: Amy Nagy is the interim director for development investment at Prosper Portland. She says taking our time, making sure that everybody is understanding of the proposed process and each step of those is really important. And Leslie Goodlow, if there's anything you want to represent from the PHB side as well.
chapters.headline: Amy Nagy is interim director for development investment at Prosper Portland
chapters.gist: Council Discusses Prosper Portland's Strategic Plan

chapters.summary: Councilor Avalos: East Portland desperately needs the resources to do the kind of community engagement so that we can build these plans. Council President Pirtle-Guinea: I am so excited for Cully and for all of the communities that are going to be able to use this model moving forward.
chapters.headline: Councilor Pirtle-Guinea applauds response to community feedback
chapters.gist: Council passes East Portland TIF Action Plan

chapters.summary: There was a note in the plan about needed community outreach. Will the Community Budget Committee be able to drive any changes that come forward, or will those be staff-driven with CLC oversight? I hope that as we move forward in, in this district in particular, decisions continue to be community-driven.
chapters.headline: There was a note in the plan about needed community outreach
chapters.gist: Community Outreach on the CLC

chapters.summary: 13 permanent affordability units and then 20 or so down payment assistance loans over the next 5 years. These are no-interest, no-payment loans, and they begin to be forgiven at year 15. Councilor: I hope that as we move forward through this plan and into the next plan, we're able to help more Culley residents buy homes.
chapters.headline: Councilor Pirtle-Guinea asks about down payment assistance
chapters.gist: Portland Housing Plan: Supporting Homeowners and Permanent Affordability

chapters.summary: Councilor Knaul: I'm going to support it today. Councilor Green: I voted no out of committee. I think we vote next week. No, we vote today.
chapters.headline: Councilor Green: I'm going to support this. I voted no out of committee
chapters.gist: Council votes on TIF Districts 1

chapters.summary: I look forward to supporting all 5 of the reappointments as well as the appointment of a new member. On the other item, I really appreciate the clarity around the overhead. I had 2 concerns and I think you've answered one.
chapters.headline: Council members discuss reappointments and overhead issues on Tuesday
chapters.gist: CLC Appointments and Overhead

chapters.summary: There's $6.7 million allocated to both grants and loans for property owners in this first 5 years. It will be up to the CLC to think through the allocations of how much of that they want it to be grants and how much they should do loans.
chapters.headline: There's $6.7 million allocated to both grants and loans for property owners
chapters.gist: Portland Economic Development Plan: Grants and Loans for Commercial Real Estate

chapters.summary: Councillor Knaul is concerned about the use of city dollars to subsidize commercial real estate. He asks for the 6 people we're about to appoint to the committee to look at how to help the people that need it, which in his view more are the tenants.
chapters.headline: Councillor Knaul expresses concern about using city funds to subsidize commercial projects
chapters.gist: Councillor Knaul on the Commercial Property Tax

chapters.summary: Almost a third of the dollars that are designated for this district are going towards administrative costs. Councillor Dunphy says he has concerns about how the city does overhead and administrative costs broadly. Councilor Avalos says he will vote no on the 5-year action plan.
chapters.headline: Almost a third of East Portland TIF dollars are going towards administrative costs
chapters.gist: Council votes no on 5-year action plan

chapters.summary: The resolution is adopted with 10 yes votes, 1 no vote, and 1 absent. Can we please call the roll on the report? Does that require a motion? The report is accepted with 12 yes votes.
chapters.headline: Councilors: Can we please call the roll on the report
chapters.gist: Council Adopts Resolution 2026-8082

chapters.summary: Council votes to appoint Eric Kress to the Prosper Portland Board of Commissioners for term to expire September 5th, 2026. Councilors Ryan and Ohrgreen say Kress showed very well in committee hearing. Council wants to enable development partner Prosper Portland to move with some nimbleness.
chapters.headline: Council approves recommendation to appoint Eric Kress to Portland-Prosper Portland board
chapters.gist: Appointment of Eric Kress to the Prosper Portland Board of Commissioners

chapters.summary: Councilor Zimmerman: It's a pleasure to support Eric on this. Councilor Kunal: Not surprised to see that support, but I also support this appointee. Has anybody signed up for testimony? No one signed up.
chapters.headline: Councilors, any further discussion on this appointment? Councilor Zimmerman supports Eric
chapters.gist: Appointment to the Central Eastside Business Advisory Board

chapters.summary: Councilor Kernall wants to know how and when to have a conversation about the governance of Prosper Portland. Council also wants to hear an update on Advance Portland 2.0. The mayor has encouraged the council to have those conversations in '26.
chapters.headline: Councilor Kernall asks question about governance of Prosper Portland
chapters.gist: Councilor Green on the Governance of Prosper Portland

chapters.summary: Colleagues, we have 12 minutes left on our agenda and 2 items left. Would you like to try to rush through this initial presentation today, or do you rather Postpone it. We will move the two remaining items to the following agenda happening on the 18th.
chapters.headline: Councilors, we have 12 minutes left on our agenda and 2 items left
chapters.gist: Council Reorders the Agenda to the Next Meeting
